title: 2025/11/20- TI
description: Thursday 20 November 2025 - Transport and Infrastructure Committee Local Government (Auckland Council) (Transport Governance) Amendment Bill Hearing of Evidence: David Boulton National Road Carriers Justin Tighe-Umbers
descriptionSummary: Transport and Infrastructure Committee. Local Government (Auckland Council) (Transport Governance) Amendment Bill. David Boulton

chunks.text: For questions. We always like that too. I'll give you a call with, uh, two minutes to go and, um, your time way you go and your time. All right. Wonderful. I have, um, slightly corrected some things, but it's more or less the same as written, so I'll just read it verbatim then, then hopefully I'll be done on time. Uh, okay. Um, your honors, I fully object to this proposed change of the legislation on the basis that the entire change has been established on falsehoods about the performance and accountability of welcoming transport in its role

as the road control authority for ko. Despite at meeting the overwhelming majority of the performance initiatives in Mayor Brown's, letters of expectation to date and at undergoing a series of restructures. As a result, mayor Brown still concluded that at was out of control, and that is a quote at has always been A CCO accountable to Auckland Council and strategic documents like the Study Center master plan and others at, has always been guided in its design standards and safety initiatives by NZT ahi.

What exactly has at done in the realm of urban planning that has not been guided by either of these two organizations or through extensive public engagement? Answers to this question have never been brought to bear revealing that this proposal is clearly a political agenda with no basis in making transport in Auckland better. And it's coming at a multimillion dollar cost to taxpayers in the form of huge amounts of administrative work.

It will be eighties third restructuring in just a few short years, and it's causing a massive degradation of morale within the organization. So people within at are being floored to continue on as if their careers are not under threat. This is all exacerbated by the fact that the decision to revoke ATS RC authority through legislative change was pushed through before there were any actual solid ideas about what would take its place or how the priority would actually, um, sorry.

How the entity would actually improve things for Auckland is dozens of staff across both AT and AC are having to fill in the gaps, the con roles specific to making sure this isn't a disaster. Meanwhile, nobody has actually articulated in any meaningful way why the very improvements touted under this bill could not have been achieved with a much less destructive internal governance initiated, um, or sorry, initiative in collaboration with Auckland Council.

Even merging parts of eighties operations back into Auckland Council could be achieved without this change. The multimillion dollar question that needs to be answered is why, why is this legislative change needed at all? There was a reason at was established in first place, and it has a lot to do with the problems of having decision making authority distributed widely across the many local wards, at least inconsistencies across the network, which inevitably exacerbates problems with congestion and could even lead to serious safety issues, particularly

as there are disagreements between experienced engineers, urban planners, and elected politicians. There is a significant amount of utility. Having the public transport authority in Auckland also be the road control authority. It makes it easy to integrate public transport services into local networks. In other words, to have a unified all network approach, which encompasses all the different competing demands, such as active modes, general traffic, buses, trains, and how they can all be accommodated within the road reserve.

David, we've got two minutes left, so, Okay. It will almost certainly be the case that making important planning decisions will become more rather than less efficient under this new RCA body. Um, I will abbreviate a little bit. Um, Auckland Transport has already committed to engaging with, in most cases and in most cases, honoring the decisions of local boards with the A two A initiative, but it also has to be recognized.

There comes a point in transportation design when technical and engineering decisions that are not a matter of public opinion, uh, sorry, are not a matter of public opinion. We proceed down this road at our peril and future generations will suffer the consequences and the costs. I think it'll take a very long time for things to recover. So my recommendation really is that we cancel the proposed legislative change and scale back the urgency of these changes, acknowledge the mistake and the huge waste of taxpayer funded human resources

required, um, and that this whole thing was launched on false pretenses by Mayor Brown and former Minister of Transport, Samian Brown. So I think we should proceed instead, recognizing that we can make these changes, you know, with, with things in situ and that that really should be the way forward. Um, there is a bit more context in my, um, submission, so I invite you guys to read it and if there's any questions, I'm happy to ask them now in the last minute.

Thank you, David. Uh, first question's from, uh, we've only got time for about one probably. So Dan, Thank you, Mr. Chair. Thank you, David, for your submission. Um, I, I'm not aware of the exact proportion, but I of course understand that there's quite a lot of negative sentiment towards at, and I I think that, um, a big part of that comes as a result of really a collective responsibility

among the council group to really, um, not, um, take accountability where accountability is due. Um, such as things like the city center master plan. There's, there's many cases where Mayor Brown has complained about works and designs in the city center, for example, which have all been, um, initiatives by Auckland Council and Auckland Transport. Thank, and that's never really been articulated.

Thank you. I should just clarify, it's 29%, so the current structure's definitely. Thanks, Dan. Not working, and this bill is aimed to fix those issues. Thank you. The issue I'll, sorry. The issue I'll just say very quickly is that it's not been articulated how the bill will actually solve the problem and increase confidence. Okay. Sorry.

No, that's fair enough. Look, David, thank you. We really appreciate your, um, your input as, as always. Thank You. Thank you very much. Thank you very much. Uh, great. We've got you down for 10 minutes as an organization. I'll give you a call with about four to go if you wanna leave some time for questions and, uh, you know, so if you just wanna highlight the key points and then, um, and then we'll go some questions.

That'll be great. Your time. Yeah, that's Great. Thank you very much. And thank you for the committee, um, taking the time to, um, hear me today. Um, now un unhelpfully, my notes have just, uh, disappeared from me. Uh, unfortunately, sorry, here we go. Yes, more technology joys.

Um, look, I was, I was briefly in front of the committee recently at the Port of Auckland as well, so I won't go into detail introducing myself. Thank you. And NRC. Um, so look, we, we support the bill. We agree with the government's position that Auckland Transport System needs to improve to support national economic goals. Um, Auckland's Auckland's unique, um, and its size and importance to New Zealand overall and unique also in the role it plays in the freight and supply chain.

You've got two of the key gateways to New Zealand here with Port of Auckland and Auckland International Airport, and also of course serves as a critical connector between north and central North Island. So how Auckland, um, manages its transport network is of critical importance, um, for the national economy and for the supply chain as well. So we, we support detail in the, um, bill, but we, there are some points of concerns particularly around in terms of striking that balance between, um, local board central governance, um, and,

and council, which I'd like to raise, uh, national Road carriers is well placed to give a view on the need for this change. We've worked very closely with Auckland Transport, um, over the years. Uh, I'm on the freight reference group at Auckland Transport, and we also support the freight working group, and it's my experience through this that the existing governance structure is below the standard needed, um, for such a critical, um, entity to the New Zealand economy in a number of important areas.

Um, it's not something I say lightly. Um, my own background is in managing large capital projects and portfolios for infrastructure companies. So I'm well placed to sort of understand the shortcomings, um, in terms of how competing demands of projects across the transport networks are managed. Uh, and I think the, the as points were raised in our submission, the existing structure limits accountability by senior leadership is one of the core areas.

So there's no individual executive member tasks with oversight of individual networks. Auckland transports managing five networks, um, but there's multiple people accountable for each individual network, which is a recipe for, um, for disaster. Um, and it means that they're not maximizing transport outcomes across the five networks as an entirety.

So one network may be improved, but it can often come at the detriment of another, um, capital planning and project delivery processes. So the structure required to be in place for portfolio management and project management and governance do not appear to be anywhere near adequate for such a complex. Um, you, you know, they're managing 2000 projects across these networks at any one time.

Um, and I can see, um, from the results that they're not managing those trade offs and interdependencies appropriately. So all of these issues will need to be addressed by the replacement government structure that Auckland Council, um, puts in place. We support the structure of governance and in terms of the balance of power between Wellington Auckland Council, so central government, Auckland Council and local boards, um, I think the, the, uh, trying to keep the, the transport function as democratically accountable

as well is very important given the, the impact that transport has on everyone's lives. Um, but what I would say is that, um, and, and so within that local boards, I think at the, the local road and connector road level, um, that's appropriate for how local boards, um, have the ability to manage those. Um, but what needs to not be lost sight of is the fact that you're dealing with a network as well.

So some of those roads will find themselves making up an actual network across Auckland. So as an example, Auckland runs a strategic freight network. It's about the only RCA in the country, I think that has a, has a defined strategic freight network, which is, um, which is excellent, that runs across some connector roads. I'm sure I'm not so sure about local roads.

It shouldn't be running across local roads in general, but what that means is if there's any decisions to be made, um, on a connector road that's part of a wider network, um, the impacts to the wider network need to be taken account as well as the local, local desires. And I think that's, the governance will need to strike the balance there. Um, and I think it's also, uh, about striking the balance in terms of what approvals are needed out of the minister as well.

So there's a real art in getting that right. You want, um, you want the large nationally significant and material projects above a particular spend to be, um, to perhaps have ministerial input, but you don't want to have sand in the gears in terms of, um, smaller decisions being made. So as, as an example, strategic freight networks currently going through a review at Auckland, it might see that some of the local roads, or I show, I should say connector roads, um, uh, now become part of the strategic freight network.

It's not clear on the current reading of the bill, but it appears that in order for that to happen, it would require ministerial sign off. Now I would say that's just that this a layer of complexity and lack of flexibility beyond what the bill's intending. And I think, um, I think the drafters just need to look closely at that to see, um, you know, when, when the minister's involvement, I'm sure the minister wouldn't appreciate having to get involved at that level of minutiae either.

Um, so the, I think they're the, they're the key points that I wish to raise, um, in terms of our submission. So now I'll pause for questions. Your timing is very good, four and a half minutes left, so I didn't quite get to that four minutes, uh, um, time, so, great. Um, colleagues, any questions? Justin? No good submissions.

Great one. Um, look, can I just ask one, uh, Justin, and it's that, um, that issue of the, the, um, the governing body and the local boards and I mean, I think effectively we've been asked by the council to be a bit more flexible to allow them to make up their mind as to how, how they do that. Got an observation on that because I think they see it as potentially quite clunky and, and cumbersome, especially if it's legislated.

Yeah, I, I, I think what you need to do, and, and I'm not sure it can be achieved within legislation, but you need to set out the principles in terms of whi which decision sets can rest with local boards comfortably, and then which ones need to go out to council. So as an example, I think we've seen, we've all seen the examples of local roads, um, wanting to have street furniture put in or, um, largely be pedestrianized or closed off.

Um, one of the caveats around that would need to be that, um, supply chain trucks can get in there. So furniture removal vehicles, for example, even if it's just a residential street and also fire and emergency heavy vehicles need to be able to get in there. Um, so it's, so you have to get into a certain amount of detail that doesn't sit comfortably in legislation, but would need to be resolved, um, within council governance.

Yeah, no, that's helpful. Thank you. Um, Simon, Uh, thank you Mr. Chair. Uh, good afternoon, Justin. Afternoon, Simon. Hello. Sorry, I, I've good, I've lost visual. Um, and that should be identifying all of those critical routes, heavy haul and over dimensional was, its its own special network coming off the port.

Is that what you're saying is that there is simply nobody in structural terms, there is nobody responsible for key decisions that's spread across a range of people, um, and nobody gets accountability or being in charge of it? Yep. That, that's right. It's, it's, it's back to just having individual executives appointed and responsible for a network that they are ultimately responsible for.

At the moment, I think it's shared across a number of individuals for each network is how it appears to be to me. And that's how compromises get made. I think just because you're moving the governance into Auckland Council doesn't necessarily solve that problem. I was gonna ask that.
